sections.heading: Why Security Matters
sections.content: Android apps handle sensitive user data — passwords, payment info, messages — making security critical. A weak app risks data theft, account takeover, and unauthorized access. Android enforces a layered model: Linux kernel sandboxing, app sandbox, permissions, secure storage, and encrypted communication. Following best practices protects users, maintains trust, and meets Play Store security requirements.

sections.heading: Key Practices
items: Use EncryptedSharedPreferences & EncryptedFile for sensitive data
Never store passwords or API keys in plain text or source code
Use Android Keystore for cryptographic keys and biometric gating
HTTPS & certificate pinning for all network traffic
Validate Intents, deep links, and WebView input
Lock down components: set android:exported=false where possible
Enable R8/ProGuard to obfuscate code and shrink resources
Harden WebViews: disable JavaScript and file access unless needed
Audit logs: never expose sensitive data

rows.description: All API calls use HTTPS; cleartext blocked
No API keys or passwords in code
Code obfuscation and resource shrinking active
No tokens, passwords, or PII in Logcat
JS & file access disabled unless needed

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Security is multi-layered: store sensitive data encrypted, use Keystore for keys, enforce HTTPS with pinning, lock down components, validate all external input, harden WebViews, obfuscate code, and audit logs. Follow the checklist to meet Play Store and user security expectations.
